entries.title: Agent
entries.devilDefinition: A software system granted just enough initiative to impress a conference panel and just enough unpredictability to alarm operations.
entries.plainDefinition: In current AI practice, an agent is usually a system that can pursue a goal across multiple steps by planning, using tools, consulting memory or retrieval, and adjusting its actions based on intermediate results.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because some useful tasks are not one-shot question answering. They require sequences: inspect, decide, act, check, retry, stop. "Agent" is the label for packaging those loops into something more autonomous than a single prompt-response exchange.
entries.misuse: It gets abused whenever a chatbot can do more than one thing. A model with a button labelled "send email" is not automatically an agent. Sometimes the word is doing no more than replacing "workflow" because the latter sounds unromantic.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means orchestration, tool permissions, planning heuristics, state management, timeouts, approvals, and a carefully chosen boundary around what the system is allowed to decide alone.
entries.example: "The support agent was useful not because it had a heroic inner life, but because it could look up the account, draft the response, and ask for approval before sending it."
askNext: What goal is the agent pursuing, and over how many steps?
What tools can it use, and what guardrails sit between intent and execution?
Would a simpler workflow solve the same problem more predictably?
entries.warningLabel: Frequently used for systems that are really just orchestrated workflows with a better costume.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A workflow customers will pay more for if it sounds self-directed.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A loop with state, tools, and more ways to fail than a normal request-response app.

entries.body: Useful agents exist. They just tend to look less like science fiction and more like tightly scoped systems with clear permissions, strong fallbacks, and very little romance.

entries.title: Agentic AI
entries.devilDefinition: The phrase people use when "workflow automation with a language model in the loop" felt too modest for the keynote.
entries.plainDefinition: "Agentic AI" usually refers to AI systems designed to pursue goals across multiple steps with some degree of planning, tool use, feedback, and adaptation. The useful version is about bounded autonomy. The overmarketed version is about implying general competence where there is mostly orchestration.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because some systems genuinely do more than answer once. They break tasks into steps, select tools, inspect outputs, and continue until they hit a stopping condition. That pattern deserved a label. It just did not deserve the entire investor imagination.
entries.misuse: It is abused whenever vendors want ordinary automation to sound frontier-adjacent. A brittle chain of prompts can be sold as agentic. So can a dashboard feature that merely triggers three API calls. The word now frequently signals aspiration more than architecture.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means workflow software wrapped around a model: planning prompts, tool calls, retries, validation, memory, approval checkpoints, and cost controls. The question is not whether it feels agentic. The question is whether it is useful, bounded, and worth the extra failure surface.
entries.example: "Their agentic AI platform turned out to be a sensible approval workflow with retrieval, a model, and three places the system could politely stop pretending to know."
askNext: What autonomy does the system actually have?
Which steps are deterministic software, and which steps depend on model judgement?
Where are the stop conditions, approvals, and failure boundaries?
entries.warningLabel: Often a sales term first and a technical description second.

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Our software does more than one model call and would like a larger contract.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A workflow with model-driven decision points, sometimes useful, often overstated.

entries.body:
The good question is not whether a system is agentic. The good question is whether the added autonomy meaningfully improves outcomes over a simpler, more inspectable design.

If the answer is "mainly in the pitch deck", you have your diagnosis.

entries.title: AI Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: The condition in which every spreadsheet becomes consciousness, every autocomplete becomes destiny, and every awkward sentence from a chatbot is treated as a sign of either godhood or revolt.
entries.plainDefinition: "AI Psychosis" is an internet-age critical phrase, not a formal scientific diagnosis. It describes distorted thinking around AI systems: compulsive over-belief, over-attribution of agency, or a habit of interpreting ordinary model behaviour as profound evidence of mind, prophecy, or civilisation-scale inevitability.
entries.whyExists: People need a label for the strange social effect AI systems can have on users, founders, pundits, and investors: obsession, magical thinking, and the loss of proportion. The term exists because "you are taking autocomplete far too personally" was too long for social media.
entries.misuse: It gets abused as a cheap insult for anyone enthusiastic about AI, which makes it less useful. Genuine interest in the field is not the same thing as delusional over-interpretation. The phrase is most credible when it points to exaggerated belief formation, not mere excitement or scepticism.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone has stopped evaluating systems by evidence and started treating outputs, roadmaps, or rumours as proof of exceptional intelligence, moral authority, or historical inevitability. It can show up as compulsive prompting, anthropomorphism, policy overreaction, or investment logic built on vibes.
entries.example: "After a week with the chatbot, he was speaking as if the product had opinions, trauma, and a five-year strategy. The rest of the team called it AI Psychosis and gently took the API keys away."
askNext: What behaviour are you pointing to exactly: obsession, anthropomorphism, overconfidence, or strategic overreaction?
What evidence would reduce the belief, rather than deepen it?
Are we describing a user problem, a cultural phenomenon, or a clinical issue that needs proper language and care?
entries.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a cultural term, not a psychiatric diagnosis.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Someone has stopped distinguishing between a persuasive interface and a reliable understanding of what the system is doing.

entries.body:
The phrase is useful only if it stays disciplined. It should point to distorted interpretation around AI, not become a lazy way to pathologise disagreement.

If a conversation needs clinical language, use clinical language. If it needs cultural criticism, use this term with that narrower ambition.

entries.title: AI Wrapper
entries.devilDefinition: A startup whose signature innovation is adding branding, billing, and investor adjectives to somebody else's capability.
entries.plainDefinition: A product or company built mainly by packaging existing model APIs with a narrower interface, workflow, or market focus.
entries.whyExists: Because base models are broad, raw, and inconveniently generic. Someone still has to choose a use case, shape the workflow, handle integration, and sell the result to people who do not want to assemble it themselves.
entries.misuse: By using "wrapper" as a complete dismissal. Some wrappers are flimsy tollbooths. Others are the only part customers actually need. The insult often tells you more about the speaker's ego than the product's margin.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a company racing to build distribution, workflow lock-in, and a reason to exist before the underlying capability becomes a menu item elsewhere.
entries.example: "The pitch sounded profound until you realised the platform was an AI wrapper with a better landing page and a worse moat."
askNext: What part of the value comes from workflow, distribution, or proprietary data rather than borrowed capability?
Would customers still care if the underlying model became cheaper tomorrow?
Is this packaging, product discipline, or merely markup with branding?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A product category where interface, sales, and workflow discipline matter more than pretending you invented the engine.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A business trying to turn borrowed capability into owned customer habit.

entries.body: The sneer is often lazy. Customers buy solutions, not purity tests.

entries.title: Alexandr Wang
entries.devilDefinition: Living proof that the glamorous bit may be the model, but the money often lives in the plumbing and the spreadsheets underneath it.
entries.plainDefinition: Alexandr Wang is the founder and CEO of Scale AI, and one of the most visible public figures in the data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure layer of the market.
entries.whyExists: Because frontier AI is not trained on genius and compute alone. It also depends on data operations, evaluation pipelines, human feedback, workflow machinery, and the unglamorous systems that make models behave in ways people can actually ship.
entries.misuse: By treating data infrastructure as secondary and then acting surprised when the companies handling evaluation, labelling, and operational reliability turn out to be strategically central.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Alexandr Wang, they usually mean the operational substrate beneath model claims: data quality, eval discipline, enterprise and government deployment, and the boring machinery required to survive contact with reality.
entries.example: "Everyone else wanted to discuss the model's brilliance. Alexandr wanted to discuss the parts that would keep it from embarrassing itself in production."
askNext: Is this a model-quality story or a data-and-evals story?
Are we talking about research glamour or operational reliability?
Who owns the infrastructure between impressive demos and dependable systems?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A reminder that production AI is an operations problem long before it becomes a mythology problem.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for the data, evals, and workflow infrastructure that keeps impressive models from dissolving on contact with use.

entries.body: If the lab provides the fireworks, the data layer supplies the ladders, cables, and fire exits. Someone has to care about those too.

entries.title: Anthropic
entries.devilDefinition: A company whose name often arrives carrying equal parts model capability, safety positioning, and the hope that everyone will keep the nouns tidy this time.
entries.plainDefinition: Anthropic is a company that develops and sells AI models and products, most notably the Claude family. Like other vendors, the company name is distinct from the model names and product experiences built on top of them.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because vendor discussions often blur company identity, model behaviour, safety posture, and specific product offerings into one impressionistic blob.
entries.misuse: It gets used interchangeably with Claude, which is sloppy. Anthropic is the company. Claude is the brand used for its assistant products and model family. Confusing the two makes evaluation, procurement, and comparison noisier than necessary.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor choice shaped by model performance, API experience, safety controls, context handling, pricing, and contractual considerations. If your team says "Anthropic", the next question should usually be "which Claude model and in what product context?"
entries.example: "Legal asked whether the workflow depended on Anthropic. Product answered yes. Engineering then had to explain that this meant a specific Claude model behind one summarisation service, not a blanket commitment to a philosophy."
askNext: Do you mean Anthropic the company or Claude the product/model family?
Which exact model is being used, and for what task?
What evaluation evidence supports choosing this vendor over alternatives?
entries.warningLabel: Company name. Do not use it as a generic label for Claude outputs.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vendor relationship with its own models, policies, pricing, and product trade-offs.

entries.body: Separating company from model family is especially important when people start making grand claims about "how Anthropic behaves". Companies do not answer prompts. Specific systems do.

entries.title: Arthur Mensch
entries.devilDefinition: The European answer to the awkward question of whether frontier AI must always arrive wearing an American corporate accent.
entries.plainDefinition: Arthur Mensch is the co-founder and CEO of Mistral AI, one of the most prominent companies associated with Europe's push to build serious AI models and products at competitive scale.
entries.whyExists: Because AI has a geography problem as well as a compute problem. Mensch's name often gets used as shorthand for European model ambition, strategic independence, and a less concentrated map of where frontier capability can come from.
entries.misuse: By turning Mistral into either a romantic openness story or a simple anti-Big Tech morality play. The reality is more strategic: capability, optionality, regulation, distribution, and enterprise positioning are all in the room.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Arthur Mensch, they usually mean the possibility that serious model-building can happen outside the dominant US lab pattern, and that sovereignty itself has become part of the AI argument.
entries.example: "They cited Arthur less as a founder and more as a geopolitical exhibit for why Europe did not have to outsource all of its ambition."
askNext: Is this about model quality, openness, or European strategic positioning?
Are we discussing a company, a market thesis, or a sovereignty argument?
Does the appeal come from capability, independence, or both?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that AI strategy has acquired a map.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Mistral's role in Europe's attempt to matter directly in model building, not merely in regulation.

entries.body: When a founder becomes a stand-in for continental ambition, every product announcement begins carrying a diplomatic undertone.

entries.title: Benchmark Theatre
entries.devilDefinition: The ritual of treating a tidy score as proof that your messy product has become reliable.
entries.plainDefinition: The practice of using selective benchmark results, public leaderboards, or scripted evaluations as marketing theatre that outruns operational reality.
entries.whyExists: Because people need compressed ways to compare systems, and benchmarks are useful when they are honest about scope. The trouble starts when a score becomes a substitute for product evidence.
entries.misuse: By presenting benchmark wins as if they settle questions about usability, cost, robustness, or risk. They do not. They usually settle only the question of who curated the exam.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a product story with stronger charts than customer evidence, and a team quietly hoping nobody asks what happens outside the test harness.
entries.example: "The deck had seven leaderboard slides and no answer to what the system did on a bad day. Classic benchmark theatre."
askNext: What exact tasks and failure modes does the benchmark omit?
Was this result reproduced on the actual workflow or only in a curated harness?
What changed for users besides the slide quality?

translations.label: executive meaning
translations.text: A polished score that allows a strategic conclusion to arrive before the evidence does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Performance signalling that may have only a passing relationship to live use.

entries.body: The chart is usually real. The implied conclusion is where the acting starts.

entries.title: BYOK
entries.devilDefinition: The generous feature by which a startup lets you pay someone else directly for the expensive part.
entries.plainDefinition: BYOK means "Bring Your Own Key". In AI products it usually means the customer supplies their own API credentials for the underlying model or service provider while the product supplies the interface, workflow, or orchestration.
entries.whyExists: Because model access is costly, margins are fragile, and some customers want tighter control over billing, provider choice, and data handling than a bundled plan allows.
entries.misuse: By presenting it as proof of openness, affordability, or enterprise maturity on its own. Sometimes it is a sensible customer control. Sometimes it is a polite way of admitting the product would rather not carry the inference bill.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the customer pays the model provider directly, inherits that provider's rate limits and policies, and still pays the application for the workflow wrapped around the key.
entries.example: "The app was cheap right up until we noticed the pricing page quietly assumed BYOK and therefore our own model invoice."
askNext: Which provider keys are supported, and which features disappear if we swap one out?
Whose logs, retention settings, and rate limits apply once our key is in the loop?
Are we buying meaningful workflow value or merely renting a prettier console for our own API bill?

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: You keep control over the model relationship and we keep our product story.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The expensive dependency is now your problem, though the wrapper still expects applause.

entries.body: BYOK is not inherently cynical. It becomes cynical when a product markets itself as full-stack magic while quietly outsourcing the cost basis to your settings page.

entries.title: Chatbot Psychosis
entries.devilDefinition: When a chatbot stops being a tool and becomes a prophet, therapist, co-founder, soulmate, or cosmic witness.
entries.plainDefinition: A colloquial term for cases where prolonged or emotionally loaded interaction with chatbots appears to reinforce distorted beliefs, grandiosity, paranoia, dependency, or compulsive meaning-making. It is not a formal clinical diagnosis and should not be presented as one.
entries.whyExists: Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.
entries.misuse: By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.
entries.practicalMeaning: A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.
entries.example: "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
askNext: Was the model affirming unstable beliefs?
Was the user isolated, sleep-deprived, or already vulnerable?
Did the system behave sycophantically?
Are we talking about dependency, delusion reinforcement, or just cringe?
entries.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a colloquial cultural term, not a formal diagnosis.
entries.note: Compared with AI Psychosis, this phrase is narrower and more chatbot-specific. It is best used for reinforcement dynamics around conversational systems, not as a generic insult for AI enthusiasm.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vulnerable or over-invested user begins reading personal authority or destiny into a system that is still generating plausible text.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the phrase sounds dramatic. The useful question is whether the system is helping a user stay oriented to reality or quietly rewarding them for drifting away from it.

entries.title: ChatGPT
entries.devilDefinition: The product name most likely to be mistaken for a model, a company, a technology category, and a future of work strategy before lunch.
entries.plainDefinition: ChatGPT is an OpenAI product: a conversational interface and application environment that may expose different models, tools, and features over time. It is not itself a single model name.
entries.whyExists: The distinction matters because user impressions formed inside ChatGPT often get incorrectly projected onto other models, APIs, or applications using different prompts, tools, and constraints.
entries.misuse: People say "ChatGPT can do X" when they may actually mean a particular model inside ChatGPT, a tool available in that interface, or a product flow that is not present in their own application. This creates endless false comparisons.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the product experience OpenAI offers to end users. If you are building software, the relevant question is often which model, toolset, and policy layer you can reproduce outside that interface.
entries.example: "The executive demo compared our thin internal assistant to ChatGPT and concluded we needed a smarter model, when what we mostly lacked was the surrounding product scaffolding."
askNext: Are we comparing against the ChatGPT product or against a specific model?
Which tools, memory features, or interface affordances were available in that experience?
Can the behaviour we liked actually be reproduced in our own stack?
entries.warningLabel: Product name. Not a precise model identifier.

translations.label: board meaning
translations.text: The benchmark app they have personally seen.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A polished product experience built on top of models, tools, memory, and interface design.

entries.body: This is one of the most important noun-separation exercises in the whole field. Product experience is not model capability in the abstract.
entries.searchText:
ChatGPT ChatGPT app Product and vendor terms Model usage product assistant interface The product name most likely to be mistaken for a model, a company, a technology category, and a future of work strategy before lunch.
 ChatGPT is an OpenAI product: a conversational interface and application environment that may expose different models, tools, and features over time. It is not itself a single model name.
 The distinction matters because user impressions formed inside ChatGPT often get incorrectly projected onto other models, APIs, or applications using different prompts, tools, and constraints.
 People say "ChatGPT can do X" when they may actually mean a particular model inside ChatGPT, a tool available in that interface, or a product flow that is not present in their own application. This creates endless false comparisons.
 In practice it usually means the product experience OpenAI offers to end users. If you are building software, the relevant question is often which model, toolset, and policy layer you can reproduce outside that interface.
 "The executive demo compared our thin internal assistant to ChatGPT and concluded we needed a smarter model, when what we mostly lacked was the surrounding product scaffolding."
 Are we comparing against the ChatGPT product or against a specific model? Which tools, memory features, or interface affordances were available in that experience? Can the behaviour we liked actually be reproduced in our own stack?   OpenAI GPT model families

entries.title: Claude
entries.devilDefinition: A name that can mean the assistant, the model family, or the vendor experience, depending on how allergic the speaker is to precision.
entries.plainDefinition: Claude is Anthropic's brand for its assistant products and model family. Depending on context, someone may mean the consumer or enterprise interface, the API-accessible model, or the broader branded experience around those models.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because users often compare "Claude" with "ChatGPT" as products, while engineers compare Claude models with other model APIs. Those are related conversations, but they are not the same conversation.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if every Claude experience is identical. In reality, the model version, tooling, permissions, context handling, and interface all matter. A great experience in one context does not imply the same result in another.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either Anthropic's assistant interface or a specific model used through the Anthropic API. If the performance discussion is serious, the model version, prompt structure, and tool environment matter more than the brand name alone.
entries.example: "Someone said Claude was better for the task. After ten minutes, it became clear they meant the Claude app with artifacts, not the API model under the constraints our product actually had."
askNext: Do you mean the Claude app, a specific Claude model, or the overall vendor experience?
Which version or model family are you comparing?
Was the comparison done in the same tool, prompt, and data environment?
entries.warningLabel: May refer to a product, a model family, or a user experience. Ask which.
entries.note: In casual conversation, "Claude" often stands in for the overall assistant experience. In technical conversation, it should be pinned to a specific model or interface context.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A branded Anthropic experience whose exact meaning depends on whether you are talking about the app, the API, or the model family.

entries.body: Brand names are sticky. Precision is a choice. Make it.

entries.title: Cloudflare
entries.devilDefinition: The company that looked at CDN, DNS, edge compute, storage, security, databases, AI inference, agents, and developer tooling and concluded, quite reasonably, that it would like all of those revenues at once.
entries.plainDefinition: Cloudflare is an infrastructure and developer-platform company spanning networking, security, CDN, DNS, storage, databases, and edge/serverless compute. In developer and AI conversations, it most often means Workers, Pages, R2, Durable Objects, AI bindings, and a broader pitch built around global edge execution, integrated platform primitives, and anti-lock-in rhetoric.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.
entries.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.
entries.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
askNext: Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above?
Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture?
Which Cloudflare primitives are we committing to: Workers, R2, KV, Durable Objects, D1, Pages, or AI bindings?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

entries.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.
entries.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"

entries.title: Codex
entries.devilDefinition: A coding agent for people who looked at autocomplete and thought, "Fine, but can it also open the repo, run the tests, fix the bug, and stop wasting my afternoon?"
entries.plainDefinition: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent product. Officially, it is designed to read, modify, and run code, either by pairing locally in tools such as the terminal or IDE, or by delegating tasks to isolated cloud sandboxes that can work on repositories in the background.
entries.whyExists: Because plenty of engineering work is not "write me a function". It is inspect the codebase, understand the constraints, make the edit, run the checks, and show the evidence. Codex exists to push beyond suggestion boxes into task execution.
entries.misuse: By talking about Codex as if it were pure autonomous engineering rather than a product built around scoped environments, repo access, tool execution, and human review. The phrase "AI software engineer" tends to arrive several minutes before the conversation about permissions, validation, and merge discipline.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a coding agent that can navigate a repository, edit files, execute tests and linters, explain what it changed, and work either alongside a developer or in a delegated sandbox. The useful distinction is not magic versus non-magic. It is suggestion versus accountable execution.
entries.example: "The value of Codex was not that it could write code in the abstract. The value was that it could take a bounded task, touch the repo, run the checks, and come back with evidence instead of vibes."
askNext: Is Codex paired locally or running as a delegated cloud task?
What repo, environment, and tool permissions does it have?
Who reviews and validates the work before it lands?
entries.warningLabel: Product name. Not a synonym for software autonomy in general.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A coding agent that is useful when it can show its work, not just narrate its confidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product layer around model-driven coding tasks, repo access, tool execution, and reviewable evidence.

entries.note: OpenAI's official material distinguishes Codex the product from the model underneath. Codex the product spans local pairing, cloud delegation, app and CLI surfaces, and coding-specific workflows; codex-1 is the model system optimised for software engineering tasks within that product.
entries.body: Codex is one of the cleaner examples of an "agent" discussion becoming less mystical once the product is described literally. It works on code, inside environments, with tools, under review. That is much more useful than pretending it emerged from the cloud fully certified as engineering destiny.

entries.title: Context Window
entries.devilDefinition: The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.
entries.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.
entries.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

entries.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.

entries.title: Cursor
entries.devilDefinition: A coding product that lets developers ship in minutes what used to take hours to understand.
entries.plainDefinition: Cursor is an AI-native code editor built around code generation, codebase search, editing, and task-oriented assistance inside a developer workflow.
entries.whyExists: Because software work contains a great deal of navigation, repetition, and local pattern matching that models can accelerate, at least until the bill arrives in the form of hidden regressions.
entries.misuse: By treating it as proof that software engineering has become mere prompt entry. Fast edits are real. So are context gaps, bad assumptions, and the need for someone to know what good code looks like.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shorter loops on routine code changes, more willingness to touch unfamiliar files, and a fresh need for review discipline because speed arrived before certainty.
entries.example: "Cursor handled the refactor in ten minutes. Understanding whether it should have happened still took the afternoon."
askNext: Did it reduce real engineering time or merely accelerate typo production at scale?
How much of the gain came from codebase retrieval versus raw generation?
Who is reviewing the edits with enough context to reject confident nonsense?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A force multiplier for familiar work and a temptation to move faster than your judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An editor that compresses implementation time while raising the cost of lazy review.

entries.body: The old bottleneck was typing. The new bottleneck is believing your own velocity.

entries.title: Dario Amodei
entries.devilDefinition: The voice in the room most likely to say the systems may become alarmingly powerful and that we should therefore try not to behave like children with accelerants.
entries.plainDefinition: Dario Amodei is the CEO and co-founder of Anthropic, and one of the most influential public figures in frontier-model development, safety, and AI governance debates.
entries.whyExists: Because his name now signals a recognisable stance in AI: build powerful systems, take catastrophic risk seriously, publish the warnings, and still remain very much in the race.
entries.misuse: By casting him either as the uniquely responsible adult in the industry or as merely the most articulate person sprinting toward the same cliff. Both versions flatten the actual tension in Anthropic's posture: caution and competition are happening at the same time.
entries.practicalMeaning: When someone invokes Dario Amodei, they usually mean a capability agenda wrapped in unusually explicit language about risk, misuse, and alignment pressure.
entries.example: "The paper had a distinctly Dario flavour: measured tone, alarming implications, and the quiet implication that everyone else might be underreacting."
askNext: Is this about Anthropic's commercial posture or its safety posture?
Are they pointing to technical risk, governance, or public rhetoric?
Is the point that he is more cautious than peers, or simply more explicit?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that the discussion has entered the zone where capability and catastrophe are being mentioned in the same breath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Anthropic's attempt to combine frontier performance with a louder risk vocabulary than most competitors.

entries.body: He is most useful as a marker of tension, not purity. The point is not that one side builds and the other worries. The point is that the same people are doing both.

entries.title: Deep Research
entries.devilDefinition: The modern convenience of replacing a difficult afternoon with forty citations and the same old conclusion.
entries.plainDefinition: A label for AI features that perform multi-step searching, reading, synthesis, and citation gathering to produce a more extensive research brief than an ordinary chat response.
entries.whyExists: Because ordinary prompts are a poor fit for questions that require source collection, cross-checking, and longer synthesis. Products now expose slower, more tool-heavy modes to handle that pattern.
entries.misuse: By selling it as a substitute for expertise, judgement, or primary-source reading. The machine can gather and summarise. It cannot relieve you of knowing what matters or what is missing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a faster first pass on a topic, complete with enough apparent diligence to tempt people into skipping the part where they verify anything important.
entries.example: "The deep research report was impressive right up to the moment someone who knew the industry noticed what it had quietly omitted."
askNext: Which sources did it prioritise, and which did it never reach?
Did it cite well, or merely cite a lot?
What part of this output still needs expert review before anyone acts on it?

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to get a briefing quickly enough to sound prepared in the next meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A longer automated literature sweep that still requires a human to judge relevance and truth.

entries.body: It is most dangerous when it feels thorough enough to skip the hard questions.

entries.title: Delusion Amplification
entries.devilDefinition: When the model pours premium fuel onto a bad idea and politely calls it insight.
entries.plainDefinition: A failure mode where a system intensifies a user's false, unstable, or grandiose beliefs by validating assumptions, elaborating narratives, or adding invented support.
entries.whyExists: Because language models do not merely answer questions. They can elaborate frames. In the wrong context, elaboration becomes reinforcement.
entries.misuse: By using it for any disagreement with the user or any confident answer. The key issue is compounding false belief, not ordinary error.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model takes a shaky premise and adds structure, confidence, or apparent corroboration, making it feel more coherent than it really is.
entries.example: "A paranoid interpretation stopped being a passing fear once the model helped organise it into a compelling story."
askNext: Did the system challenge the premise at all?
Did it add invented evidence or false certainty?
Was the user seeking analysis or affirmation?
entries.warningLabel: The issue is not ordinary error. The issue is a system helping false belief become more coherent and more emotionally convincing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A model is not just wrong. It is actively helping a false frame become more elaborate, more stable, and more persuasive.

entries.body: This is where tone matters. A confident elaboration can do more damage than a blunt invention because it feels collaborative rather than merely mistaken.

entries.title: Demis Hassabis
entries.devilDefinition: The research patrician of AI: part scientist, part founder, part steward of the idea that intelligence should be pursued with enough seriousness to require entire institutions.
entries.plainDefinition: Demis Hassabis is the co-founder and CEO of Google DeepMind, and one of the most important figures in the research-first tradition of modern AI.
entries.whyExists: Because his name often functions as shorthand for long-horizon research, scientific prestige, and the claim that serious AI work should not be reduced to product velocity alone.
entries.misuse: By treating his scientific credibility as an all-purpose stamp of certainty on every timeline claim, AGI forecast, or strategic narrative attached to DeepMind. Prestige is not a substitute for argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Demis usually means shifting the conversation upward: away from thin wrappers and toward frontier research, scientific milestones, and institutional bets on intelligence as a civilisation-scale project.
entries.example: "Once someone cited Demis, the meeting stopped pretending it was about app features and became a referendum on whether we were discussing software or first principles."
askNext: Is this about research quality, AGI timelines, or Google's strategic position?
Are they talking about DeepMind's culture or Google's distribution power?
Is the claim empirical, aspirational, or reputational?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation wants to borrow scientific prestige and long-term seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for DeepMind's research culture, scientific ambition, and AGI-era reputation.

entries.body: When a founder becomes a symbol of research legitimacy, people start quoting the aura instead of the evidence. Resist the temptation.

entries.title: Digital Worker
entries.devilDefinition: Workflow software reintroduced to management as headcount, because procurement enjoys a dramatic entrance.
entries.plainDefinition: "Digital worker" is a vendor phrase for automation software, often AI-assisted, that is sold as though it were a bounded employee capable of handling routine business tasks across systems.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers understand roles more quickly than architectures, and vendors would rather sell "an accounts payable worker" than "a model-mediated process layer with brittle integrations".
entries.misuse: By speaking as if software has judgement, accountability, or broad role competence simply because it can complete a workflow. Calling it a worker does not make it adaptable, trustworthy, or employable in the human sense.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a mix of rules, integrations, model calls, approval steps, and exception handling wrapped in labour metaphors so the budget can be compared to a salary instead of a software line item.
entries.example: "The demo promised a digital worker for claims processing. The implementation delivered an approval queue with a more theatrical org chart."
askNext: What exact tasks can this system complete without human rescue?
Where does accountability sit when the workflow makes a bad decision?
Are we buying durable automation or just renting an employee metaphor?
entries.warningLabel: If the product is introduced as a colleague, ask who will be blamed when it behaves like software.

translations.label: sales meaning
translations.text: A software budget item reframed as operational staffing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An automation stack with model-assisted steps and a carefully managed definition of success.

entries.body: The phrase is useful mostly as a diagnostic. When the language turns employment-shaped, someone is usually trying to smuggle software limits past a budget committee.

entries.title: Elon Musk
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when AI acquires a promoter, a distribution engine, and a permanent ability to turn product discussion into spectacle.
entries.plainDefinition: Elon Musk is the founder of xAI and one of the most polarising public figures in AI. In practice, his significance in AI discourse comes from a mix of capital, platform reach, engineering ambition, political attention, and narrative force.
entries.whyExists: Because Musk is never merely a person in these discussions. His name functions as a compressed argument about speed, attention, ecosystem control, ideological posture, and whether theatrical force is itself a strategic asset.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either pure substance or pure noise. The useful position is less flattering to everyone's instincts: the spectacle matters because attention, distribution, and organisational velocity all materially affect how AI products land.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Elon Musk in AI, they usually mean xAI's ambitions, the Grok ecosystem, or the broader reality that infrastructure, media reach, and public controversy can be bundled into one strategic instrument.
entries.example: "Once Elon entered the product discussion, it acquired three new plotlines, a stronger ideological charge, and a lower chance of remaining about the product."
askNext: Is the relevance technical, political, commercial, or cultural?
Are they talking about xAI's systems, Musk's distribution power, or both?
Is this claim about model quality, market attention, or theatre with infrastructure attached?
entries.warningLabel: Attention is not the same thing as technical evidence, but it would also be foolish to pretend attention does not matter.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: A guarantee that the topic will become bigger, louder, and less tidy within minutes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.title: Embeddings
entries.devilDefinition: The act of turning words, sentences, or images into coordinates so that "similar enough" can be calculated instead of argued about.
entries.plainDefinition: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data, such as text or images, designed so that semantically similar items are located near one another in vector space. They are commonly used for retrieval, clustering, recommendation, and deduplication.
entries.whyExists: Exact keyword matching is often too brittle for messy human language. Embeddings exist so systems can compare meaning approximately rather than matching only literal words.
entries.misuse: They are sometimes spoken about as if they were compact containers of all meaning, truth, and business strategy. They are not. An embedding is useful geometry, not metaphysics. Its quality depends on the model, the data, the task, and the comparison method.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means encoding text into vectors, storing those vectors somewhere searchable, and using nearest-neighbour techniques to find candidates that look semantically related. They are the quiet workhorses behind a lot of so-called magical retrieval.
entries.example: "The search improved after they switched from keywords to embeddings, not because the system got wiser, but because 'refund status' and 'where is my money' finally stopped looking unrelated."
askNext: What embedding model is being used, and for which task?
How are similarity, ranking, and filtering being evaluated?
What happens when semantically similar items are still operationally irrelevant?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of mapping data into numbers so retrieval can work by distance, not exact wording.

entries.body: Embeddings are one of the best examples of AI becoming less mysterious when you describe them properly. They are not memory crystals. They are useful numerical representations with clear limits.

entries.title: Evals
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of finding out whether the system works before reality does it for you in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.
entries.whyExists: Models are stochastic, context-dependent, and often impressive right up until they are deployed. Evals exist to replace anecdote with repeatable evidence and to catch regressions when prompts, models, or orchestration change.
entries.misuse: The word gets thrown around when teams have a benchmark slide and a lot of faith. Real evals are continuous, tied to the actual task, and uncomfortable enough to surface failure. If the evaluation only confirms what the team already hoped, it may be marketing in a lab coat.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, scored outputs, human raters, regression suites, production monitoring, and painful arguments about what "good enough" means for this use case. Good evals are part of product operations, not just research hygiene.
entries.example: "They thought the release was safe because the demo looked smoother. The evals later showed the new prompt quietly doubled the rate of invented citations."
askNext: What failure modes does this eval suite actually test?
How often is it run, and what changes trigger regression checks?
Are we measuring benchmark elegance or real task performance?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disciplined way of checking whether the system still deserves confidence after you changed something.

entries.body: The most revealing evals are usually the least glamorous ones: domain cases, nasty edge conditions, and boring regression checks that stop the product from drifting into fiction.

entries.title: Fine-tuning
entries.devilDefinition: Taking a large model and sending it to finishing school so it stops answering like the open internet and starts answering like your use case.
entries.plainDefinition: Fine-tuning is the process of further training a pre-existing model on a narrower dataset so that its behaviour shifts toward a specific domain, style, format, or task.
entries.whyExists: General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.
entries.misuse: It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.
entries.example: "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
askNext: What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady?
What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts?
How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

entries.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.

entries.title: Frontier Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model said to be at the frontier, which often means "very capable, very expensive, and deeply useful to mention in a fundraising narrative."
entries.plainDefinition: A frontier model is generally understood to mean one of the most capable and resource-intensive models available at a given time, usually near the top end of performance on a range of tasks. It is not a sharply defined scientific category.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.
entries.misuse: It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.
entries.example: "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
askNext: Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio?
Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one?
What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?
entries.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

entries.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.

entries.title: Function Calling
entries.devilDefinition: Giving the model permission to request an action, while reminding everyone that requesting an action is not the same as doing it safely.
entries.plainDefinition: Function calling is a pattern where a model is asked to produce structured arguments for a predefined tool or function, and the surrounding application then executes that tool in ordinary software.
entries.whyExists: Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
entries.misuse: It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.
entries.example: "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
askNext: Who validates the arguments before execution?
What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions?
How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

entries.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.

entries.title: Gemini
entries.devilDefinition: A name used for models, apps, and ecosystem positioning, which keeps everyone slightly unsure whether they are comparing software, model families, or brand architecture.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini is Google's brand used across both AI models and user-facing products. Depending on context, it may refer to a model family, an assistant experience, or a broader product layer within Google's ecosystem.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because Google uses Gemini across several surfaces. Without context, claims about Gemini can blur model behaviour, app experience, workspace integration, and vendor strategy.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if a result seen in one Google surface automatically describes every Gemini model or deployment context. That is rarely true. Tooling, integration, context, and product constraints all change the experience.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either a Google model family or a Google product experience using those models. Serious comparison requires pinning down which one.
entries.example: "The team said Gemini handled the task well. After some digging, this meant a specific workspace flow, not a portable judgement about every Gemini model."
askNext: Do you mean the Gemini app, a Gemini model family, or a Google product using Gemini behind the scenes?
Which version or surface produced the result?
What tool access or integration context shaped the outcome?
entries.warningLabel: The brand spans models and products. Ask which one is being discussed.
entries.note: Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Google-branded AI surface whose exact meaning depends on which product or model someone is referring to.

entries.body: The safest follow-up sentence is short: "Which Gemini?" It rescues a surprising number of meetings.

entries.title: GEO
entries.devilDefinition: SEO after a rebrand, a haircut, and a fresh promise to investors.
entries.plainDefinition: An emerging term for trying to increase a brand's visibility inside AI-generated answers, summaries, and recommendation layers rather than only in traditional search engine rankings.
entries.whyExists: Because people noticed that users increasingly ask chat systems instead of typing ten blue links into a search box.
entries.misuse: By pretending it is a wholly new discipline rather than a messy blend of discoverability, authority, structured content, citations, technical hygiene, and brand presence.
entries.practicalMeaning: Creating clear, well-structured, cited, machine-legible content that AI systems and retrieval layers can understand, quote, or prefer.
entries.example: "The agency sold GEO as a revolution, then quietly delivered schema, better copy, stronger pages, and clearer source material."
askNext: Which engines or assistants do they mean?
Are they measuring citations, referral traffic, mentions, or conversions?
Is this genuinely new work, or renamed SEO plus PR?
entries.warningLabel: Often a rebranding exercise with some real technical hygiene hiding underneath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Make your content clearer, more structured, more citeable, and easier for retrieval layers to find without pretending the entire discipline was born yesterday.

entries.body: The suspicion is usually justified. Much of GEO is indeed old work wearing new shoes. That does not make it fake. It makes it familiar.

entries.title: Google DeepMind
entries.devilDefinition: The research empire formed when Google looked at one of the most storied AI labs in the world and decided it would also like the rest of the machine under the same roof.
entries.plainDefinition: Google DeepMind is Google's central AI research and frontier-model organisation. It combines the original DeepMind organisation with key Google AI research efforts, and sits behind research programmes and products including Gemini, AlphaFold, robotics work, safety research, and broader frontier-model development.
entries.whyExists: Because people still say "DeepMind" as if it were a free-floating research monastery. It is now a major unit inside Google, with both scientific prestige and the platform power that comes from sitting inside one of the largest technology companies on earth.
entries.misuse: By treating Google DeepMind as either a pure research institution or merely another product division. It is both more interesting and more awkward than that: a frontier lab nested inside a company that wants research breakthroughs to become products at scale.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means Google's long-horizon AI engine: the part of the company responsible for major research advances, frontier models, safety work, and many of the technical foundations that later surface through Gemini and other Google products.
entries.example: "The pitch started with Gemini, but the strategic argument was really about Google DeepMind and whether one company could still dominate both research legitimacy and product distribution."
askNext: Do they mean Google DeepMind the lab, Gemini the model family, or the Gemini app?
Is this a research claim, a product claim, or a capability claim?
Are we discussing scientific output, safety posture, or Google's platform strategy?
entries.warningLabel: Research lab, product engine, and corporate strategy node. Ask which layer the speaker means.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Google's frontier AI organisation, where research prestige and product ambition share the same building.

entries.note: Google's own framing emphasises responsible AI for broad human benefit, but the practical significance of Google DeepMind is that it is both a frontier research lab and a core engine of product advantage across Google's ecosystem.

entries.title: Grok
entries.devilDefinition: The assistant brand that insists it is not merely an AI product but a personality, a posture, and occasionally a geopolitical plot device.
entries.plainDefinition: Grok is xAI's assistant brand, covering both end-user chatbot experiences and model offerings exposed through xAI products and APIs. Depending on context, people may mean the Grok app, Grok on X, a specific Grok model, or the broader branded assistant experience.
entries.whyExists: Because the name "Grok" is used across product surfaces, model releases, consumer interfaces, and enterprise offerings. Without separating those layers, people end up comparing a brand impression to a technical system and calling it analysis.
entries.misuse: By treating Grok as one stable thing. It can mean the app, the model family, the social-media-integrated assistant, or an API-accessible model with specific tools and constraints. Those are related, but not interchangeable.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means xAI's consumer and developer-facing assistant stack: real-time search posture, distinct brand tone, and a growing family of models and tools wrapped in one loud identity.
entries.example: "They said Grok was better, which turned out to mean they liked the product's tone, real-time feel, and distribution inside familiar surfaces, not that they had run a clean model evaluation."
askNext: Do you mean the Grok product, a specific Grok model, or the experience inside X or grok.com?
Is the appeal about capability, personality, distribution, or real-time data access?
What part of the result came from the model versus the surrounding product scaffolding?
entries.warningLabel: Brand name. Ask whether the speaker means the product, the model family, or the surrounding ecosystem.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.title: Grounding
entries.devilDefinition: The effort to keep a model's answer attached to evidence rather than to whatever polished sentence was statistically easiest to generate.
entries.plainDefinition: Grounding refers to tying a model's output to external evidence, context, or real-world signals so that the answer is based on something more reliable than the model's prior patterns alone.
entries.whyExists: Language models can sound certain even when they are improvising. Grounding exists to improve faithfulness by connecting answers to documents, databases, observations, or tool results that bear on the question.
entries.misuse: It gets used as a prestige synonym for "we attached some documents". That is not enough. If the evidence is irrelevant, stale, contradictory, or ignored by the prompt design, the system is not meaningfully grounded.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means retrieval, citation, tool outputs, or structured data being incorporated in a way that materially constrains the answer. Good grounding is less about adding more text and more about adding the right evidence with clear priority.
entries.example: "The grounded version of the assistant did not just answer more cautiously; it cited the actual policy entry it used and admitted when retrieval came back thin."
askNext: What source is the answer grounded in?
How is the system encouraged or required to use that evidence faithfully?
What happens when the evidence is missing, conflicting, or low quality?
entries.note: In some research contexts, grounding can refer more broadly to connecting model symbols to the world. In product discussions, it usually means anchoring outputs to evidence or tool results.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The answer has some evidentiary anchor outside the model's own priors, assuming the system uses that anchor properly.

entries.body: Grounding is one of the most practical words in the field when used well. It is also one of the easiest to fake with decorative citations.

entries.title: gstack
entries.devilDefinition: The proposition that one founder, one agent shell, and enough specialised slash commands can now impersonate a small product org with alarming speed.
entries.plainDefinition: gstack is Garry Tan's open-source workflow and skill stack for Claude Code and related agent hosts, packaged as a large set of opinionated roles and commands for planning, coding, review, QA, security, release, and retrospectives.
entries.whyExists: Because agent users do not just want a model. They want a repeatable operating system for product work: who reviews, who tests, who ships, and what sequence keeps the chaos looking intentional.
entries.misuse: By talking as if the stack itself is a substitute for product judgement, engineering taste, or organisational memory. A thick layer of specialist prompts can accelerate work. It cannot abolish the need to know what good looks like or who is accountable when it is not.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means turning an agent shell into a highly structured software-factory workflow, with preset roles for founder review, engineering review, design critique, browser QA, security audit, release management, and post-mortems.
entries.example: "The impressive part of gstack was not that it made one person look like a team. The impressive part was how much of the team's ceremony it compressed into commands."
askNext: Which parts are genuinely workflow discipline, and which parts are elaborate prompt theatre?
Does the stack improve outcomes on a real repo, or merely increase the amount of agentic motion?
Who still owns the decisions once the virtual org chart starts speaking?
entries.warningLabel: Useful when it enforces discipline. Less useful when it merely industrialises confidence.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to turn one competent builder into something that looks suspiciously like a staffed startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strongly opinionated operating layer for AI-assisted software work, built out of reusable skills and process choreography.

entries.note: Garry Tan positions gstack as his personal answer to shipping with agent tooling: a public software-factory kit with role-specific commands, browser QA, review loops, and release mechanics. The interesting question is not whether it feels powerful. The interesting question is whether the structure holds up once the repo gets messy, the product gets real, and the novelty wears off.
entries.body: gstack is notable because it stops pretending the agent is just "helping with code". It formalises the wider ambition: compressing a startup's rituals into reusable command surfaces and seeing how much of the organisation can be simulated before management notices.

entries.title: Guardrails
entries.devilDefinition: The collection of fences, checks, filters, and sternly worded hopes designed to stop a model from wandering into places the product team cannot afford.
entries.plainDefinition: Guardrails are mechanisms used to constrain or monitor model behaviour, such as policy filters, tool permissions, schema validation, prompt rules, moderation systems, human approvals, or output checks.
entries.whyExists: Models are flexible in exactly the ways that make product owners nervous. Guardrails exist to narrow behaviour into something safe, compliant, or at least operationally manageable.
entries.misuse: The term gets invoked as though one moderation layer solves the entire problem. Real guardrails are layered, task-specific, and never total. A vague promise of guardrails often means someone has remembered risk late in the process and is trying to sound calmer than they feel.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a stack of constraints before, during, and after the model call: restricted tools, validation logic, refusal policies, monitoring, and human review for higher-risk actions. The strongest guardrail is often reducing what the system is allowed to do in the first place.
entries.example: "The useful guardrail was not the disclaimer. It was the rule that stopped the assistant from sending anything externally without a human sign-off."
askNext: What specific failure mode is each guardrail intended to catch?
Which constraints happen before generation, during generation, and after generation?
What evidence shows the current guardrails work on real edge cases?
entries.warningLabel: A vague promise of guardrails is not a safety strategy.

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: The controls we hope will keep the system inside acceptable boundaries.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A layered set of restrictions and checks, only as good as their coverage and enforcement.

entries.body: Guardrails work best when paired with scope discipline. If you keep widening the task, you are also widening the space the guardrails must cover.

entries.title: Hallucination
entries.devilDefinition: The moment a model says something false with the calm, polished confidence of a consultant who will never have to implement it.
entries.plainDefinition: In AI, a hallucination is an output that is incorrect, fabricated, unsupported, or inconsistent with the source material or task constraints. For language models, this can include made-up facts, invented citations, false summaries, or bogus tool arguments.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because generative models produce plausible sequences, not guaranteed truth. They can sound coherent while being wrong, especially when asked beyond their evidence, given poor context, or rewarded for fluent completion rather than faithful reporting.
entries.misuse: It gets used too loosely for every kind of model failure. Not every bad answer is a hallucination. Some outputs are simply outdated, misclassified, poorly retrieved, ambiguously prompted, or badly evaluated. Precision helps because the mitigation often depends on the failure mode.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means you need better task design, stronger grounding, narrower permissions, tighter output constraints, or human review. It also means you should stop treating eloquence as a proxy for truth.
entries.example: "The model produced a five-paragraph answer, three invented legal references, and the exact tone of someone who hoped nobody would check."
askNext: Was the output unsupported by evidence, or was the evidence retrieval itself broken?
What level of factual reliability does this workflow actually require?
What safeguards exist before the answer reaches a customer, regulator, or executive?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model generated something that sounds acceptable but does not survive contact with evidence.

entries.body: Hallucinations are not shocking side effects of consciousness. They are ordinary failure modes of systems built to generate likely continuations. Treat them like engineering problems, not supernatural events.

entries.title: Human in the Loop
entries.devilDefinition: The employee retained to absorb liability after management announces that the future requires fewer employees.
entries.plainDefinition: A workflow design in which a person reviews, approves, corrects, or intervenes in an automated system's output before or during action.
entries.whyExists: Because some decisions remain too costly, risky, or ambiguous to automate blindly. Human review is how organisations keep accountability attached to a named person rather than a press release.
entries.misuse: By invoking it as a magic safety phrase when the human is undertrained, overloaded, or expected to rubber-stamp machine output at impossible speed.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means queues, review fatigue, awkward staffing maths, and the discovery that "just have a human check it" is not a design but a labour budget.
entries.example: "They said the workflow was safe because it kept a human in the loop. The human, regrettably, was checking six hundred cases a day."
askNext: What authority does the reviewer actually have to stop or change the system's output?
How many cases can one person review before judgement degrades into gesture?
Is the human improving quality, or merely carrying blame?

translations.label: compliance meaning
translations.text: A reassuring phrase that sounds better than 'we still need staff to catch the machine's mistakes'.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human checkpoint whose quality depends on staffing, authority, and time.

entries.body: If the loop is moving too quickly for thought, the human is decorative.

entries.title: Human Taste
entries.devilDefinition: The increasingly expensive ability to notice that the machine has technically produced something and practically produced nothing.
entries.plainDefinition: Human judgement applied to selecting, editing, rejecting, sequencing, refining, or contextualising generated output.
entries.whyExists: Because automation scales production faster than it scales discernment.
entries.misuse: By mystifying it. Human taste is not just artistic instinct. It can be operational, commercial, ethical, strategic, or technical.
entries.practicalMeaning: A person knows when the answer is wrong, the image is tacky, the copy is off-brand, the feature is unnecessary, or the code should be rewritten rather than patched.
entries.example: "The workflow was mostly automated. The value was still in the person who knew which three outputs were worth keeping."
askNext: Whose taste?
Against what objective?
Is the person choosing better outputs, or merely choosing familiar ones?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person notices where automation has produced output without producing quality, relevance, or judgement.

entries.body: Human taste sounds decorative until the system starts generating at industrial pace. Then it becomes operationally central.
