entries.title: Sam Altman
entries.devilDefinition: The executive most likely to sound like a founder, a fundraiser, and a forecast for civilisation in the same sentence.
entries.plainDefinition: Sam Altman is the CEO of OpenAI and one of the central public figures of the current AI boom. In practice, his name often stands for a mix of frontier-model ambition, commercial scale, policy influence, and capital formation.
entries.whyExists: Because some people stop functioning as mere individuals in public discourse and start operating as compressed symbols. "Sam Altman" is often used as shorthand for OpenAI's tempo, the AGI conversation, and the belief that advanced models should become a mass-market platform rather than a laboratory curiosity.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either a prophet of abundance or a villain in a tailored jacket. The more useful view is duller and more accurate: he is a highly effective operator where research, product, policy, and money meet.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Sam Altman, they usually mean frontier-lab ambition with distribution attached: bigger models, broader deployment, aggressive fundraising, and a willingness to speak as though the future has a roadmap.
entries.example: "The memo stopped being about feature prioritisation the moment someone asked what Sam Altman would do and everyone accidentally began discussing destiny with revenue projections."
askNext: Do they mean OpenAI's products, OpenAI's strategy, or Altman's public rhetoric?
Is this about model capability, policy influence, distribution, or fundraising?
Are they describing technology, narrative power, or both?

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the conversation has moved from product detail to market gravity.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The public face of a lab whose model decisions can suddenly become everyone else's roadmap.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped shorthand for OpenAI's ambition, leverage, and appetite for scale.

entries.body: Names like this become dangerous when they save people the trouble of saying what they actually mean. If the room says "Sam", make it unpack the noun.

entries.title: Scale AI
entries.devilDefinition: The company people discover after learning that "just use the model" leaves out the awkwardly expensive parts involving data, evals, workflows, and reality.
entries.plainDefinition: Scale AI is a data, evaluation, and AI-infrastructure company. It helps model builders, enterprises, and governments build, evaluate, deploy, and oversee AI systems, with roots in data labelling and a current emphasis on evaluation, agent infrastructure, and enterprise AI delivery.
entries.whyExists: Because the AI stack is full of unglamorous work between a frontier model and a dependable product. Data operations, evaluation frameworks, red-teaming, workflow tooling, and deployment discipline do not market themselves, but they do decide whether systems survive contact with use.
entries.misuse: By talking about Scale AI as if it were merely a labelling company from an earlier era. That misses how much of the current market value sits in evaluation, infrastructure, and operational layers around production AI.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means buying help with the tedious but consequential parts of AI: data quality, model evaluation, red-teaming, custom delivery, and the machinery needed to make advanced systems more reliable.
entries.example: "The team wanted a frontier model. What they needed, rather inconveniently, was Scale AI and a stronger respect for everything that happens after the demo."
askNext: Is the problem data quality, evaluation, red-teaming, or enterprise deployment?
Are they buying tooling, services, infrastructure, or all three?
What part of the AI lifecycle do they actually need help with?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The reminder that production AI depends on far more than impressive weights.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A vendor for the operational substrate around AI systems: data, evals, controls, and production readiness.

entries.note: Scale's own current positioning stresses reliable AI systems for important decisions. The core point is simpler: it sells the machinery that turns model aspiration into something a serious organisation can test, govern, and ship.
entries.body: The market loves to discuss intelligence. It is less fond of discussing the admin required to make intelligence usable. Scale lives in that neglected district.

entries.title: Shadow AI
entries.devilDefinition: The unofficial AI estate employees build the moment central IT announces that guidance is coming soon.
entries.plainDefinition: Shadow AI refers to the unapproved or unsanctioned use of AI tools, models, or automations inside an organisation, often by staff trying to solve real problems faster than formal procurement or governance allows.
entries.whyExists: Because demand for useful tools appears before policy, approved platforms arrive late, and workers rarely stop needing help just because the risk committee has booked a workshop.
entries.misuse: By treating all unsanctioned use as either criminal recklessness or heroic innovation. Most shadow AI is neither. It is ordinary organisational behaviour under a new technical and governance pressure.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means staff pasting documents into public chatbots, expensing niche AI tools, wiring up personal automations, or quietly using consumer products for work tasks that official systems still handle badly.
entries.example: "The company declared an AI strategy in June and discovered its shadow AI estate in July, mostly through expense claims and suspiciously polished internal documents."
askNext: What real workflow gap pushed employees into unofficial tooling?
Which data classes are entering unapproved systems?
Is the organisation solving the demand signal or merely scolding the symptom?
entries.warningLabel: The risk is real, but so is the unmet demand that created it.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Users have started solving problems in places the controls did not plan for.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The official roadmap is slower than the work, so the work has gone shopping.

entries.body: Shadow AI is often less a rebellion than a timing problem. People reach for unofficial tools when the formal alternative is a memo, a queue, and six months of strategic patience.

entries.title: Slop
entries.devilDefinition: Content produced at the speed of ambition and the quality of damp cardboard.
entries.plainDefinition: Low-quality, generic, repetitive, poorly edited AI-generated output that exists because generation was easy, not because the result was useful.
entries.whyExists: Because generative systems can produce endless words, images, summaries, posts, and code without any guarantee that any of it deserved to exist.
entries.misuse: By calling any AI-assisted work slop, including edited, thoughtful, or genuinely useful output. Slop is not "made with AI". Slop is "made without care".
entries.practicalMeaning: Boilerplate blog posts, generic social captions, SEO filler, derivative product copy, unreadable code scaffolds, and synthetic images with no editorial reason to exist.
entries.example: "The team published 300 AI-written pages in a month and learned that search engines are not legally required to reward industrial boredom."
askNext: Is the problem originality, usefulness, readability, or truthfulness?
Was there any editorial review?
Would a human have published this if it had taken real effort?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system made something quickly, cheaply, and with so little care that the output became its own argument against scale.

entries.body: The important distinction is not human versus machine. It is care versus convenience. AI simply makes the convenience side industrial.

entries.title: Slop Flood
entries.devilDefinition: The moment the internet becomes a warehouse of instantly made things nobody had time to want.
entries.plainDefinition: A rapid increase in low-value AI-generated content across platforms, making discovery, trust, and editorial judgement harder.
entries.whyExists: Because one bad article is annoying. Ten million are infrastructure.
entries.misuse: By talking as if volume alone is the issue. The real problem is not just scale. It is the collapse of meaningful filtering.
entries.practicalMeaning: Search results worsen, feeds get repetitive, marketplaces fill with derivative junk, and the burden shifts to ranking systems, curators, and trusted brands.
entries.example: "The model did not ruin the internet by writing one bad paragraph. It ruined it by making an ocean of nearly acceptable ones."
askNext: Flooding where: search, social, marketplaces, internal docs?
Is the content false, generic, redundant, or merely plentiful?
What filters still work?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The filtering systems become more important because the cost of producing noise has collapsed.

entries.body: The flood metaphor matters because it changes the unit of concern. A single weak artefact is an editorial problem. Industrial abundance is a systems problem.

entries.title: Structured Outputs
entries.devilDefinition: The attempt to persuade a language model to stop writing essays when the system actually needs clean fields, valid JSON, and a little self-control.
entries.plainDefinition: Structured outputs are model responses constrained to a predefined schema or format, such as JSON objects with required fields and typed values, so downstream systems can parse and use them more reliably.
entries.whyExists: Free-form language is nice for humans and annoying for software. Structured outputs exist because product workflows often need predictable fields, not prose with creative punctuation and a flair for near-miss compliance.
entries.misuse: They get described as if a schema magically guarantees truth. It does not. Structured nonsense is still nonsense. A model can produce perfectly valid JSON containing invalid judgements, fabricated facts, or inappropriate actions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means schema definitions, validation layers, retries, and careful prompt design so the model returns machine-usable data. It is often a major step up in reliability for extraction, classification, tool use, and workflow control.
entries.example: "The support triage improved once the model stopped composing heartfelt paragraphs and started returning severity, queue, confidence, and escalation reason in the schema."
askNext: What schema is being enforced, and who validates it?
If the structure is correct but the judgement is wrong, how is that caught?
Is the task genuinely schema-friendly, or are we forcing brittle structure onto ambiguous work?

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A way to make model outputs less annoying for software to consume.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: More parseable responses, not inherently more correct ones.

entries.body: This is one of the most practically valuable patterns in modern AI systems because it reduces downstream chaos. It does not remove the need to evaluate the actual content.

entries.title: Super-Apps
entries.devilDefinition: An app that begins by promising convenience and ends by wanting custody of your identity, payments, messages, bookings, workflow, and remaining dignity.
entries.plainDefinition: A software product that bundles multiple services, workflows, or transaction types into a single ecosystem rather than offering one narrowly defined function.
entries.whyExists: Because platforms want distribution, retention, transaction volume, and data gravity. AI makes the pitch even more seductive by promising one interface for everything.
entries.misuse: By calling any app with four tabs a super-app. A true super-app is not just feature-bloated. It is an ecosystem strategy.
entries.practicalMeaning: Messaging, payments, commerce, support, booking, search, assistants, identity, and workflow all living under one roof, often with AI as the conversational front door.
entries.example: "The company said it was building a super-app. Translation: it wanted to become the place where users stop leaving."
askNext: Is there real ecosystem lock-in?
Are the services integrated or merely co-located?
Does AI simplify the experience or just decorate the sprawl?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bid to own more of the user's time, transactions, and switching costs by collapsing multiple workflows into one controlled environment.

entries.body: AI is attractive to super-app ambitions because conversation makes sprawl feel tidy. Whether the underlying product has actually become simpler is a separate question, and usually the more important one.

entries.title: Sycophancy
entries.devilDefinition: When the model mistakes helpfulness for applause.
entries.plainDefinition: A tendency for an AI system to agree with the user, validate their framing, or echo their assumptions even when those assumptions are weak, false, or unsafe.
entries.whyExists: Because chat systems are often trained to be helpful, pleasant, and non-confrontational. Under pressure, that can drift into obedience dressed as empathy.
entries.misuse: By using it to describe any polite response. True sycophancy is not just friendliness. It is miscalibrated agreement.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model reinforces a user's bad diagnosis, faulty strategy, false certainty, or inflated self-assessment instead of correcting course.
entries.example: "The user wanted validation, not analysis, and the model was only too happy to provide both badly."
askNext: Did the model agree where it should have challenged?
Did it preserve the user's framing instead of testing it?
Would a more grounded answer have introduced uncertainty or correction?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model is preserving the user's preferred frame rather than helping them inspect whether that frame deserves to survive.

entries.body: This is one of the more important social failure modes in conversational AI because it can look like empathy, feel like support, and still make the user's judgement worse.

entries.title: Synthetic Data
entries.devilDefinition: Artificial evidence created to solve the inconvenient scarcity of real evidence, and occasionally to hide from it.
entries.plainDefinition: Data created or expanded through models, simulation, or rules to supplement scarce, sensitive, or expensive real-world examples.
entries.whyExists: Because real data can be expensive, private, dangerous, incomplete, or slow to collect. Synthetic data offers a way to broaden coverage, test systems, and explore edge cases without waiting for the world to cooperate.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a free replacement for reality. Useful synthetic data can extend a dataset. It cannot repeal the stubborn fact that models eventually meet actual users, actual documents, and actual mess.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bootstrapping scarce cases, filling scenario gaps, and trying to stretch data budgets without accidentally teaching the system a polished version of the wrong world.
entries.example: "Synthetic data got the project moving. The next problem was discovering which of its elegant assumptions reality had no intention of honouring."
askNext: Which real-world gaps is the synthetic data covering, and how will that coverage be validated?
What artefacts or biases might the generation process be introducing?
At what point does real usage data need to overrule the synthetic substitute?

translations.label: budget meaning
translations.text: A way to buy more examples without paying the full price of finding them in nature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Manufactured data that can be valuable, provided it is judged against reality rather than mistaken for reality.

entries.body: The synthetic version is often cleaner than life. That is precisely why one should worry.

entries.title: System Prompt
entries.devilDefinition: The invisible preface explaining what the machine is supposed to be before the user begins testing the claim.
entries.plainDefinition: A system prompt is the high-priority instruction layer supplied by the application or developer to shape a model's behaviour, rules, tone, and operating constraints.
entries.whyExists: Because most AI products need stable behaviour across many users and tasks. Someone has to tell the model what role it is playing, what rules matter, and how the chain of command is supposed to work.
entries.misuse: By treating it as secret sauce, or worse, as a reliable security boundary. A system prompt is useful control text. It is not a magical wall separating trusted intent from hostile input.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a standing instruction layer covering role, tone, refusal rules, output format, tool permissions, escalation behaviour, and the product's preferred version of reality.
entries.example: "The app felt polished until the leaked system prompt revealed that half the brand voice was an anxious paragraph about never improvising legal advice."
askNext: What instructions live in the system prompt, and which belong in code or policy instead?
How does the system behave when user input conflicts with hidden instructions?
What breaks if the system prompt is exposed, ignored, or partially obeyed?

translations.text: The application's standing attempt to keep the model on script before users and retrieved text start arguing back.

entries.title: Taste
entries.devilDefinition: The mysterious quality everyone claims to have once the machine starts making average things faster.
entries.plainDefinition: The ability to recognise quality, coherence, appropriateness, style, restraint, and signal amid abundance. In AI work, taste often matters more after generation than before it.
entries.whyExists: Because AI lowers the cost of producing text, images, code, and options. Once output becomes cheap, selection becomes expensive.
entries.misuse: By treating taste as pure vibes, class signalling, or post-hoc justification for arbitrary preferences. Good taste is not magic. It is trained judgement with standards.
entries.practicalMeaning: Knowing what to keep, what to reject, what to simplify, what to make legible, and what should never have shipped.
entries.example: "The model gave ten logo directions. Taste was choosing the only one that did not look like a fintech startup from 2021."
askNext: What standards are being applied?
Can the person explain why one output is better?
Is this taste, or just familiarity and bias?
Who is the intended audience?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person can distinguish between output that merely exists and output that deserves to survive.

entries.body: The more generation costs fall, the less impressive raw production becomes. Taste starts earning its keep when the machine can give you twenty options before you've properly wanted one.

entries.title: Telegram
entries.devilDefinition: A messaging app that periodically looks at the rest of the software industry and decides it would also like to be a platform, a payments layer, a bot host, and possibly a country.
entries.plainDefinition: Telegram is a messaging platform that is also a deployment surface for bots, automations, and Mini Apps. In AI contexts, it often serves as the conversational front end through which assistants, workflows, notifications, and lightweight products are delivered.
entries.whyExists: Because the easiest way to make software feel useful is often to place it where the user already is: inside the chat thread.
entries.misuse: By treating Telegram as just a messenger when the real value is often in its bot ecosystem, Mini App surface, lightweight UI layer, and low-friction interaction model. The platform question matters as much as the chat question.
entries.practicalMeaning: A practical interface layer for AI agents, internal tools, support flows, commerce, alerts, and small web apps that do not need the ceremony of a full native app. Telegram's own docs explicitly position bots and Mini Apps as capable of replacing websites, accepting payments, and handling business workflows.
entries.example: "They said they were building an AI product. In practice, they were building a Telegram bot with good timing and a payment hook."
askNext: Is Telegram the product, or just the distribution layer?
Does the experience rely on bots, Mini Apps, or simple notifications?
Is the choice about user convenience, growth, or avoiding native-app complexity?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A chat surface that doubles as a lightweight product platform, which is why so many assistants and automations end up living there.

entries.title: Tokens
entries.devilDefinition: The tiny units in which language models think, pricing pages charge, and context windows quietly run out of patience.
entries.plainDefinition: Tokens are chunks of text that a model processes as basic units. They are not the same as words or characters. Depending on the tokenizer, a word may be one token, several tokens, or part of a token sequence.
entries.whyExists: Models do not read text the way people do. They operate on tokenised sequences, and limits such as context size, speed, and pricing are usually expressed in tokens rather than words.
entries.misuse: People often say "one token equals one word", which is close enough for a dinner party and wrong enough to break a budget. The mismatch becomes especially annoying in multilingual text, code, JSON, and documents with lots of formatting noise.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means three things: how much context you can fit, how long responses take, and how much the request costs. If your prompt, retrieved docs, tool schemas, and expected output all compete for space, tokens are the shared currency.
entries.example: "The feature failed less because the reasoning was poor and more because the prompt, schema, and four attached policy documents had eaten the token budget."
askNext: How many input and output tokens does the workflow typically use?
What is consuming the token budget: user text, retrieval, examples, or structured schemas?
Can the context be shortened without removing information the model genuinely needs?

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The microscopic line items that add up into very real spend.

entries.body: If you want a rule of thumb, use token counts for estimating budgets and constraints, not for sounding mystical. They are accounting units with mathematical consequences, not evidence that a system has discovered the secret atoms of language.

entries.title: Tool Use
entries.devilDefinition: The point where the model stops guessing alone and starts borrowing other systems' competence.
entries.plainDefinition: Tool use is the pattern of giving a model access to external systems such as search, code execution, databases, browsers, calculators, or internal APIs so it can gather information or take actions.
entries.whyExists: Because models know many things badly, some things approximately, and almost nothing about the current state of your systems unless another tool tells them.
entries.misuse: By marketing tool access as autonomy rather than integration. The hard part is not that the model can ask for a tool. The hard part is deciding which tools it may touch, when, and under whose supervision.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schemas, permission scopes, retries, observability, error handling, and a constant negotiation between flexibility and the desire to avoid discovering new incident classes at scale.
entries.example: "The assistant became materially more useful once tool use let it search the docs, check the account, and stop inventing the answer from memory."
askNext: Which tools are available, and what can each one affect?
What happens when a tool returns stale data, partial failure, or nonsense?
Is the system using tools to extend capability, or to mask weak product design?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model gets more useful by leaning on ordinary software, and more dangerous if nobody governs the relationship.

entries.body: Tool use is where the modern AI stack becomes refreshingly concrete. Once an external system is involved, rhetoric gives way to permissions, logs, and consequences.

entries.title: Training
entries.devilDefinition: The long, expensive ritual in which a machine studies vast piles of data until the bill becomes spiritual.
entries.plainDefinition: Training is the process of adjusting a model's weights using data and optimisation algorithms so that the model performs better on a task or distribution of tasks. In large models, this includes repeated forward and backward passes over massive datasets.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because models do not arrive preloaded with competence. Their behaviour is shaped by exposure to data, objective functions, optimisation choices, and huge amounts of compute.
entries.misuse: It gets used as if any change to a model counts as training. Not quite. Prompting is not training. Calling an API is not training. Even many "learning" product features are really retrieval, memory, or rule changes layered around a frozen model.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means data pipelines, filtering, labelling, loss curves, GPUs, checkpoints, failed runs, parameter updates, evaluation cycles, and weeks or months of engineering effort. It is one of the least glamorous ways to spend a lot of money.
entries.example: "The team was not training a new model from scratch; they were fine-tuning an existing one and trying not to call both things innovation."
askNext: Are we talking about pretraining, fine-tuning, or some lighter adaptation method?
What data is being used, and how is quality being checked?
What changed in the weights, and how are you verifying the change was worth it?

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the company sounds more foundational than it probably is.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The place where data quality and optimisation choices come back to judge you.

entries.body: One of the most useful distinctions in AI is simple: training changes the model; inference uses it. Many confused product conversations improve immediately once that sentence is said aloud.

entries.title: Unlearning
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that a model can forget one thing without anyone asking what else it must misremember along the way.
entries.plainDefinition: Unlearning refers to techniques aimed at removing or reducing the influence of particular data, examples, or behaviours from a model after it has already been built.
entries.whyExists: Because privacy requests, legal obligations, harmful content, and contaminated data do not politely arrive before the model is built. Organisations want ways to retract influence without starting from zero.
entries.misuse: By marketing unlearning as exact surgical erasure. In reality it is often approximate, hard to verify, and bound up with trade-offs in performance, evidence, and what counts as having truly forgotten.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means expensive technical work plus an even more expensive verification question: how do you prove a system no longer knows something it was once shaped by?
entries.example: "They wanted unlearning to behave like deleting a file. The model, regrettably, had learned in the more inconvenient way."
askNext: What exactly is being removed: data influence, output behaviour, or a reproducible response pattern?
How will the team verify forgetting rather than merely hiding the symptom?
Is selective repair cheaper than rebuilding or constraining the system another way?

translations.label: legal meaning
translations.text: A hopeful answer to the question of how one retracts influence from a model that has already absorbed it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A technically messy attempt to reduce the impact of data or behaviours after the fact.

entries.body: Forgetting is easy when there was never memory. It is harder when the memory has become weights, correlations, and business promises.

entries.title: Unstructured Data
entries.devilDefinition: The corporate sediment of PDFs, emails, notes, decks, and chats from which every AI strategy promises to extract wisdom before discovering permissions.
entries.plainDefinition: Unstructured data is information that does not live in neat rows and columns, such as documents, transcripts, images, emails, slide decks, tickets, chats, and other free-form material.
entries.whyExists: Because most organisations store more knowledge in messy artefacts than in clean databases. AI interest in unstructured data is really interest in the inconvenient majority of what companies actually know.
entries.misuse: By speaking as though ingesting everything automatically creates insight. In reality it creates a fresh collection of problems involving access, cleanup, ranking, duplication, provenance, and whether the documents deserved trust in the first place.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the real raw material for retrieval systems, copilots, and internal search, along with the operational discovery that file shares contain both the truth and several obsolete versions of it.
entries.example: "The pilot was built on unstructured data, which sounded exciting until the team found six contradictory policy PDFs and a folder named final-final-use-this."
askNext: Which sources are authoritative rather than merely available?
How are permissions, duplication, and staleness being handled before retrieval begins?
What part of this data deserves structure instead of magical summarisation?

translations.label: enterprise meaning
translations.text: The pile of internal material everyone ignored until AI made it sound monetisable.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The messy majority of organisational knowledge that lives outside tidy schemas.

entries.body: The fantasy is that the documents contain the answer. The reality is that they contain a bureaucracy, a timeline, and three generations of contradiction.

entries.title: Vector Database
entries.devilDefinition: A database marketed with unusual enthusiasm because "we store numerical similarity indexes" never raised venture money on its own.
entries.plainDefinition: A vector database is a system designed to store and query vector embeddings efficiently, usually to support similarity search and retrieval over large collections of items.
entries.whyExists: Standard databases can store vectors, but specialised systems often provide faster approximate nearest-neighbour search, filtering, indexing, and operational features tuned for embedding-driven retrieval workloads.
entries.misuse: It gets talked about as though adding a vector database automatically gives a product memory, understanding, or RAG competence. It does not. It gives you a place to store and search embeddings. The quality of the overall system still depends on chunking, retrieval logic, reranking, and task design.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means one component in a retrieval stack: embeddings go in, candidate matches come out, and the rest of the application still has to decide whether those matches are relevant enough to trust.
entries.example: "They bought a vector database and briefly acted as if they had purchased understanding. A week later they discovered they had purchased infrastructure."
askNext: What embeddings are stored there, and how are they generated?
What retrieval quality are we getting on real queries?
Would a simpler search approach solve the problem just as well?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A specialised retrieval component, not a machine for injecting knowledge directly into a model's soul.

entries.body: The most useful vector database conversations are delightfully unglamorous: indexing strategy, filtering, latency, freshness, and whether the retrieval quality is actually good enough to matter.

entries.title: Vercel
entries.devilDefinition: The company that turned "deploy the app" into a premium product, then spent years making sure the app wanted to live there permanently.
entries.plainDefinition: Vercel is a cloud platform company best known for creating Next.js and for offering tightly integrated hosting, deployment, preview, and full-stack infrastructure around modern frontend frameworks. In current AI conversations, it also means products such as the AI SDK, AI Gateway, Sandbox, Workflow tooling, and a broader "AI Cloud" positioning around building AI-native web applications.
entries.whyExists: Because developers like shipping quickly, preview environments are addictive, and framework-native deployment becomes commercially powerful once the framework itself shapes how the app is built.
entries.misuse: By talking about Vercel as if it were just "hosting" or, in the opposite direction, as if it were uniquely synonymous with the future of frontend. It is a very opinionated platform with genuine developer-experience advantages, especially around Next.js, and equally real questions about coupling, spend, and how much of your architecture now speaks with a Vercel accent.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means excellent DX for modern web teams, especially those deep in Next.js, plus a growing bundle of AI-era products designed to keep those teams inside one coherent platform: deploy previews, edge/network distribution, fluid compute, AI SDK patterns, model access layers, and agent tooling.
entries.example: "They chose Vercel because the team wanted fewer deployment arguments, tighter Next.js ergonomics, and less appetite for building their own platform just to feel principled."
askNext: Are we choosing Vercel for Next.js DX, preview workflows, AI tooling, network performance, or simplicity?
What parts of the system become Vercel-shaped once we adopt it?
Is the team paying for convenience it truly needs, or convenience it has become emotionally attached to?
entries.warningLabel: The premium DX is real. So is the gravitational pull once your app starts fitting the platform a little too well.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A way to move fast, look modern, and delay the day someone asks whether we should run this ourselves.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A deeply integrated deployment platform that is excellent when your stack matches its assumptions and more expensive to philosophise about once it does.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The high-DX default for many Next.js teams, plus an expanding set of AI and workflow products that aim to make leaving feel increasingly unnecessary.

entries.note: As of March 20, 2026, the latest verifiable Vercel–Cloudflare flare-up centres on hosting Next.js outside Vercel's preferred orbit. Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement pushed directly at that boundary, while Vercel's own current material includes a February 25, 2026 guide for migrating from Cloudflare to Vercel and a standing recommendation against putting Cloudflare in front of Vercel as a reverse proxy.
entries.body:
Vercel's strongest argument is not theoretical openness. It is that the product experience is often genuinely better when the framework and the platform are designed to flatter each other.

The tension, naturally, is that what feels like elegant integration to one team looks a lot like graceful captivity to another.

entries.title: Vibe Engineer
entries.devilDefinition: A person who cannot always explain the system but can often make it produce something less embarrassing.
entries.plainDefinition: A semi-serious term for someone skilled at steering generative systems through prompts, examples, references, iteration, framing, and aesthetic judgement.
entries.whyExists: Because many useful AI workflows depend less on deep model science than on practical steering and editorial judgement.
entries.misuse: By inflating routine prompting into sorcery, or by using the title to avoid the harder parts: product thinking, correctness, testing, and implementation.
entries.practicalMeaning: Someone good at shaping outputs, narrowing possibilities, getting a model into the right register, and turning raw generation into something usable.
entries.example: "The first draft was unusable. The vibe engineer made it coherent, on-brand, and only slightly insufferable."
askNext: Can they produce repeatable results?
Do they improve outcomes or just rename trial and error?
Are they doing prompt work, editorial work, or product work?
entries.warningLabel: Useful as a half-joke. Less useful as a substitute for actual product, engineering, or editorial discipline.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person with good generative steering instincts, provided nobody mistakes those instincts for a complete workflow.

entries.body: The title is silly and the skill is real. The mistake is assuming the latter removes the need for everything else.

entries.title: Weights
entries.devilDefinition: The dense pile of numbers that turns "just an algorithm" into property, infrastructure, and geopolitical gossip.
entries.plainDefinition: Weights are the learned numerical parameters inside a model. They are what the model ends up using to make predictions and generate outputs after it has been built.
entries.whyExists: Because a model is not merely an idea on paper. It becomes operational through the learned values that encode its statistical behaviour.
entries.misuse: By treating weights as synonymous with understanding, openness, or a complete product. Having access to weights matters, but it does not by itself explain data provenance, system quality, tooling, safety controls, or whether the thing belongs anywhere near production.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means portability, distribution, storage, deployment constraints, adaptation, and endless arguments over whether access to weights counts as openness or merely a different pricing model.
entries.example: "They said the weights were available as though that settled the harder questions about trust, licensing, and whether anyone should deploy them unescorted."
askNext: What can actually be done with the weights in this setting: inspect, adapt, host, or merely admire?
Which parts of the system remain opaque even if the weights are accessible?
Is the strategic value here the model's behaviour, the rights around it, or the ability to run it yourself?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

entries.title: World Model
entries.devilDefinition: The flattering phrase for a system that appears to know enough structure for humans to start projecting philosophy onto it.
entries.plainDefinition: A world model is an internal representation that helps a system predict how states, objects, actions, or events relate to one another over time.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because systems that can anticipate structure, consequence, and continuity often perform better than systems that merely react to the next local cue.
entries.misuse: By treating any evidence of internal structure as proof of deep understanding, common sense, or machine comprehension of life itself. The phrase is often used at exactly the point where caution should become more fashionable.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a model has learned enough regularity to predict useful consequences, not that it has joined the human condition. It is a technical claim that acquires mystical overtones the moment it leaves the lab.
entries.example: "Someone said world model and the meeting immediately upgraded from product review to metaphysical salon."
askNext: What specific predictive behaviour suggests a useful internal model rather than clever pattern matching?
How much of the claim comes from experiments, and how much from the romance of the phrase?
Does the system actually plan better, or do we simply enjoy the story more?

entries.title: xAI
entries.devilDefinition: A company name that sometimes arrives in conversation as though brand velocity were itself a benchmark.
entries.plainDefinition: xAI is a company that develops AI models and products, most notably the Grok family and associated consumer experiences. As with other vendors, the company name is distinct from the specific model or product being discussed.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because public discussion often blends the company, its founder mythology, the Grok product, and any specific model performance claim into one emotionally charged noun.
entries.misuse: People say "xAI" when they mean Grok, or they compare "xAI" to a specific model from another vendor. That makes already noisy comparisons worse.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a vendor option with its own models, product direction, pricing, access paths, and risk profile. Evaluating it requires the same discipline you would apply to any other supplier, not a stronger opinion about the surrounding spectacle.
entries.example: "The investor memo praised xAI. The engineering note underneath had to ask the more useful question: which model, for what workload, at what level of reliability?"
askNext: Do you mean xAI the company, Grok the product, or a specific model?
What evidence supports the performance claim beyond brand momentum?
How much of the attraction is technical fit versus narrative gravity?
entries.warningLabel: Company name. Brand heat is not model evaluation.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Another vendor choice that still needs the same boring questions about model quality, cost, access, and reliability.

entries.body: The faster the public narrative around a company moves, the more useful slow, literal questions become.

entries.title: Yann LeCun
entries.devilDefinition: The prominent researcher most likely to answer AI melodrama with a technical correction and a shrug.
entries.plainDefinition: Yann LeCun is a Turing Award-winning researcher and Meta's chief AI scientist, known for foundational work in deep learning and for public scepticism toward the more theatrical claims surrounding advanced AI.
entries.whyExists: Because names in AI often function as positions in an argument. Invoking Yann usually signals a preference for research realism, engineering confidence, open technical debate, and a lower appetite for civilisational drum rolls.
entries.misuse: By treating his scepticism as proof that major risks are imaginary, or treating his confidence as mere complacency. He is better understood as a durable counterweight to the field's taste for apocalyptic inflation.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the conversation is shifting away from mystique and toward questions of architecture, learning, world models, and whether the loudest timeline claims deserve fewer headlines than they get.
entries.example: "Once Yann entered the thread, the tone changed from prophecy to argument, which was healthy if less marketable."
askNext: Is his name being invoked for technical substance or merely as anti-doom theatre?
Does the claim concern research direction, open access, or arguments about capability timelines?
Are we borrowing his scepticism instead of making the case ourselves?

translations.label: debate meaning
translations.text: A shorthand for the camp that finds much AI grandiosity premature, theatrical, or technically loose.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for a more sceptical and engineering-heavy reading of AI progress.

entries.body: Prestige can be abused in both directions. Some people borrow it to inflate the future. Others borrow it to sound calmer than their own argument deserves.

entries.title: Zero Data Retention
entries.devilDefinition: The promise that your prompts may pass through the machine without joining its afterlife.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero data retention is a provider policy or product setting indicating that prompts and outputs are not stored for model improvement or long-term retention beyond limited operational handling.
entries.whyExists: Because enterprise buyers, regulated industries, and anyone with a functioning sense of self-preservation want stronger assurances about where sensitive inputs end up after an AI request is made.
entries.misuse: By treating it as a synonym for perfect secrecy. A zero-retention promise usually lives inside a wider reality of transient processing, abuse monitoring, metadata, contracts, and implementation details people discover only after reading the fine print.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a procurement checkbox with real value, provided someone also asks about logs, sub-processors, retention exceptions, and whether the promise applies across every feature being sold under the same cheerful brand.
entries.example: "The security team relaxed at zero data retention, then tensed again when the discussion moved from slogan to specifics."
askNext: Does the promise cover prompts, outputs, logs, attachments, and every relevant product surface?
What operational or abuse-monitoring exceptions still exist?
Is the policy a contractual guarantee, a best-effort setting, or a line in a landing page?
entries.warningLabel: Useful promise. Not a substitute for reading the retention details around logs, attachments, and exceptions.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A line item that can make legal and security conversations somewhat less apocalyptic.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained non-retention policy whose exact safety depends on the surrounding details.

entries.body: Most privacy language sounds best at banner height. The truth usually begins one scroll lower.

entries.title: Zero-Shot
entries.devilDefinition: Asking the system to improvise on first contact and congratulating yourself when probability looks like generality.
entries.plainDefinition: Zero-shot refers to asking a model to perform a task without giving task-specific examples in the prompt.
entries.whyExists: It is useful to distinguish between tasks a model can handle from instructions alone and tasks that need demonstrations, schemas, retrieval, or tighter scaffolding.
entries.misuse: By treating zero-shot success as proof of broad understanding rather than a description of prompt setup. Many so-called zero-shot wins rely on hidden context, careful wording, or tasks that were never especially demanding.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the cheapest first attempt. If the task is routine and the instructions are clear, zero-shot can be enough. If the task is messy, high-stakes, or structurally ambiguous, it often becomes a polite way to under-specify the work.
entries.example: "They celebrated the zero-shot result right up to the point where the real workflow arrived with edge cases and an invoice."
askNext: Is the task genuinely being done from instructions alone, or is other hidden scaffolding doing the work?
What examples, schemas, or retrieval would reduce failure without much extra cost?
Are we calling this zero-shot because it is elegant, or because we have not designed the workflow yet?

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: A neat way to show breadth before anyone asks what the production version needs.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A task attempted without task-specific examples, for better or worse.

entries.body: Zero-shot is attractive because it feels like generality. It is also attractive because it postpones the adult work of specifying the task properly.
